In Tucson, the university grew while the old Mexican neighborhoods were demolished for urban renewal.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Rapid growth strains water resources in the desert — groundwater pumping unsustainable
Residential segregation confines minorities to specific neighborhoods
Urban renewal destroys historic Mexican-American and Black neighborhoods in Tucson and Phoenix
Indigenous communities lack basic infrastructure — water, electricity, healthcare
narrative_hooks: A family from Cleveland arrives in Phoenix in 1955, turns on the air conditioning in their new house, and decides they will never shovel snow again
A Navajo elder watches Phoenix sprawl across the desert from the rim of a mesa, knowing that the water being pumped to fill those swimming pools comes from an aquifer that will not last forever
A Motorola engineer in Phoenix designs a circuit that will help put a man on the moon, working in an air-conditioned lab in a city that would not exist without that same technology
A Mexican-American family in Tucson watches their barrio demolished for urban renewal and wonders where the planners expect them to go

key_events: Barry Goldwater, Arizona senator, runs for president (1964), defining modern American conservatism
Glen Canyon Dam completed on the Colorado River (1966), creating Lake Powell
Phoenix becomes one of the fastest-growing cities in America
Navajo and other indigenous nations intensify legal battles for water and land rights
Arizona's electronics and aerospace industries expand rapidly
Civil rights activism grows — Phoenix and Tucson desegregate public accommodations
1960s.economic_conditions: Arizona's economy boomed. Electronics, aerospace, and defense industries grew alongside tourism, real estate, and retirement migration. Phoenix's metropolitan area sprawled across the desert, creating a car-dependent landscape of subdivisions, shopping centers, and freeways. Agriculture remained significant but its share of the economy shrank. Water — always Arizona's most critical resource — became an increasingly urgent political issue as growth outstripped supply. The Central Arizona Project was authorized (1968) to bring Colorado River water to Phoenix and Tucson.
dominant_industries: electronics and aerospace (Motorola, Hughes, GE, Sperry Rand)
1960s.migration_patterns: Arizona's population grew to 1.77 million. Phoenix's explosive growth continued, with the metro area passing 800,000. Sun City — the first large-scale retirement community — opened in 1960, launching the retirement migration that would reshape Arizona demographics. The tech sector attracted educated workers from across the country. Mexican immigration, both legal and undocumented, supplied labor for agriculture, construction, and services. Indigenous populations continued growing on the reservations.
1960s.social_context: Barry Goldwater's 1964 presidential campaign defined Arizona's conservative identity — small government, individual liberty, military strength, and resistance to federal regulation. The civil rights movement reached Arizona, where de facto segregation in housing, schools, and public accommodations was challenged. The Navajo and Hopi nations fought for water rights and sovereignty. The state's growth raised fundamental questions about sustainability in the desert — questions that remain unanswered.
1960s.daily_life_snapshot: In Sun City, retirees from Chicago and Minneapolis played golf in January while their old neighbors shoveled snow. At the Glen Canyon Dam site, engineers completed the massive concrete structure that flooded Glen Canyon — one of the most beautiful places on earth — to create Lake Powell. In Phoenix, freeways knitted together a sprawling metro area of identical subdivisions. On the Navajo Reservation, a new generation of leaders trained in American law schools prepared to fight for their people's rights in American courts.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Glen Canyon Dam floods Glen Canyon, destroying a landscape of extraordinary beauty (1966)
Groundwater depletion threatens Arizona's long-term water supply
Racial discrimination in housing and employment persists despite civil rights gains
Extreme summer heat kills vulnerable residents, especially the elderly and outdoor workers
narrative_hooks: A river guide takes his last trip through Glen Canyon before the dam closes the floodgates, photographing a paradise that will be underwater by next year
Barry Goldwater, speaking at the Republican convention in 1964, declares that 'extremism in the defense of liberty is no vice' — defining Arizona conservatism for generations
A retiree from Minneapolis moves to Sun City, buys a house with a pool, and wonders if the desert can really support all these people
A Navajo lawyer, educated at a university that once tried to erase her culture, files a water rights claim that will take decades to resolve
A Phoenix developer breaks ground on another subdivision in the desert, confident that there will always be enough water — a confidence his grandchildren will question
